Both sides have issues, though being a single father and raising your child alone makes them better adjusted than those with single mothers, because of a lack of a father figure. replies.author: @Vherstinae
replies.text:
@Khazandar It's not "must" but it's definitely "more likely to." More specifically, children raised by single fathers - regardless of through divorce or death in the family - were virtually indistinguishable in academic, economic and social achievement from well-adjusted children from two-parent households. Meanwhile children of single mothers - including those who later remarry, and regardless of divorce, death or birth out of wedlock - tend to do far worse, much more likely to drop out of school, to become criminals, and to have been abused by their remaining parent.
Yes, some fathers are abusive. Mine certainly was. But it doesn't change the fact that, in general, fathers are key to a child's development into a functional adult not destructive to themselves or others.

These crash and burns always remind of what Sir Christopher Lee said about young actors and the dangers they face. 

"So many of them are stars  before they even make their first film. And you know when you are looking at them on the screen that they are looking in the mirror and it's the beginning of a lifelong romance. Because that's what they want: to be rich and famous immediately. Which is terribly sad and extremely dangerous. The most dangerous thing that a young actor or actress can do is believe their own publicity." 
- Sir Christopher Lee
